title: Chapter 2: Voice from the Abyss
paragraphs: Humidity. It was the first thing Avotu felt when the red fog of the panic attack began to recede, leaving behind only the metallic taste of blood on his tongue. The air in the catacombs was thick and stagnant, smelling of ancient dust and some sweet, chemical rot. He sat on the cold floor, leaning his back against a cracked marble tomb, and mechanically wiped his face with a gauntlet. The scarlet blood of his memories had vanished, leaving only gray ash.
He was alone. Again. Sivarus's legacy—this city of Agdash—was dying, and he, its greatest defender, was losing his mind in the dark.
Suddenly, a low, gurgling sound echoed through the vaulted corridor. It wasn't the screech of a harvester or the roar of a demon. It was a voice. Raspy, wet, and undeniably intelligent.
'You... you are still here, little spark,' the voice wheezed. 'Still trying to hold together the fragments of a broken mirror.'

Avotu stood up, his movements fluid and deadly despite the exhaustion. Black flame flared on his head, casting long, dancing shadows on the walls. He drew Nature's Blade. The meteoric iron hummed in anticipation.
From the shadows emerged a creature that had once been a high demon of the Void. Now, it was a melting mass of dark matter and jagged bone, its eyes glowing with a faint, dying violet light.
'Zakhan's filth,' Avotu spat, the black flame turning a sharp, aggressive orange at the edges. 'Why shouldn't I end your misery right now?'

The demon let out a sound that might have been a laugh, if its lungs weren't filled with void-tar. 'End it? I am already at the end. We all are. I didn't come to fight you, fire elf. I came to tell you the truth you're so afraid to see.'
Avotu paused, the tip of his blade barely inches from the creature's pulsating throat. 'What truth?'

'The portals... they aren't doors,' the demon whispered, its voice fading. 'They are wounds. The world isn't being invaded; it's being erased. Every time you close one, you just move the rot somewhere else. Your brothers... the other disciples... they knew. That's why they left.'
The word 'left' hit Avotu harder than any physical blow. 'They didn't leave. They were lost. I saw them fall.'
'Did you?' the demon's eyes flickered and went out. 'Or did you just see what you needed to stay sane?'

The creature collapsed into a puddle of black sludge, which instantly evaporated, leaving only a faint scent of ozone. Avotu stood in the darkness, the demon's words echoing in the silence of his own mind.

In the lower levels of the catacombs, the group from home had found a small, semi-dry chamber. They had built a tiny fire from some ancient wooden crates they found. The light was flickering, but it was enough to see each other's faces.

Ravan was meticulously sharpening a piece of metal he'd found, his large hands surprisingly steady. 'My son would have loved this place,' he said suddenly, a sad smile touching his lips. 'He's obsessed with Minecraft. He'd be building a fortress right now.'
Yuri looked up from the map he was trying to sketch on a piece of dusty parchment. 'Minecraft doesn't have the smell of rotting demons, Ravan.'
'Detail,' Ravan shrugged. 'He's a tough kid. Like his dad.'
Anar, who had been leaning against a wall with his eyes closed, suddenly tensed. 'Something's coming.'
Alexander was already on his feet, his rusty crossbow leveled at the entrance. 'Wait. It's not a monster.'

A figure stepped into the light. It was tall, clad in scorched armor, with a head of hair that wasn't hair at all, but a low, smoldering black flame. The strangers from another world stared at the fire elf, and the fire elf stared back.
Avotu saw five beings who radiated no mana, yet their presence was as solid as the stone around them. They were an anomaly. A variable he hadn't accounted for.
'Who... what are you?' Alexander asked, his voice steady despite the sweat on his brow.
Avotu looked at the man who stood at the front, protecting his friends. He saw a mirror of himself—a leader trying to keep his people alive in a world that wanted them dead.
'I am the shadow of a world that no longer exists,' Avotu replied in a voice that sounded like grinding stones. 'And you... you are pieces that don't belong in this puzzle.'
